excel_structure.notes: Excel file contains 1 test case with complex multi-step scenario embedded in a single Steps field. The Steps field contains approximately 21 distinct test steps with expected results embedded inline. Test case involves HP Instant Ink subscription pause plan functionality with visual and functional verification of billing cycle period card, progress bars, and page usage tracking.

test_case_metadata.title: Billing Cycle Period card
test_case_metadata.description: Test case to verify the Billing Cycle Period card display and behavior on Print and Payment History page when a subscription plan is paused, including verification of complimentary pages progress bar, additional pages tracking, tooltips, and page usage information display across different printing scenarios.
test_case_metadata.sub_module: Print and Payment History - Pause Plan
test_case_metadata.author: Nitish

custom_fields.section: Print and Payment History page with 25/50/100/300/700 pages plan
custom_fields.section_hierarchy: Instant Supply > Regression Test > 2 - Medium > Dashboard - Extended > Pause Plan > Print and Payment History page with 25/50/100/300/700 pages plan
custom_fields.analysis_status: To be discussed: Specific Ink printer? 4.Click the Pause Plan button on CIT modal or "Pause Instant Ink" link to trigger the pause plan. Manual execution for pages on overview to be performed and verified the same

preconditions.raw_text: Enroll the Ink printer with 50 pages plan or higher.
preconditions.interpreted_meaning: A physical or virtual HP Ink printer must be enrolled in the HP Instant Ink subscription service with a plan that provides 50 pages per month or a higher tier plan (100, 300, or 700 pages). This enrollment must be completed and active before the test begins.

implied_components.reason: Need API or UI automation to enroll a printer with specific plan tier
Need printer ID, plan type (50/100/300/700 pages), and enrollment credentials
Need to verify printer is successfully enrolled with correct plan
May need to navigate enrollment flow if done via UI
Enrollment may take time to process

preconditions.raw_text: Make sure the Subscription has no free months info.
preconditions.interpreted_meaning: The HP Instant Ink subscription for the enrolled printer must not have any promotional free months applied. The subscription should be in a standard paid state without any trial or promotional period active.

implied_components.reason: Need to check subscription details via API to confirm no free months
Need to assert that free months field is null or zero
Need test data representing a subscription in standard paid state

preconditions.raw_text: Make sure the subscription is "subscribed" status.
preconditions.interpreted_meaning: The HP Instant Ink subscription must be in an active 'subscribed' status (not paused, not cancelled, not expired, not pending). This is the normal active state before triggering the pause plan action.

implied_components.reason: Need to query subscription status via API
May need utility to reset subscription to subscribed state if it's in another state

preconditions.raw_text: Click the Pause Plan button on CIT modal or "Pause Instant Ink" link to trigger the pause plan.
preconditions.interpreted_meaning: Before starting the test steps, the user must trigger the pause plan action by either clicking a 'Pause Plan' button on a CIT (Customer Interaction Tool) modal dialog or clicking a 'Pause Instant Ink' link. This action changes the subscription from 'subscribed' to 'paused' status and is a required setup step for the test.

implied_components.reason: Need page object for CIT modal if pause is triggered via modal
Need page object for main dashboard where Pause Instant Ink link may be located
Need locator for Pause Plan button on CIT modal
Need locator for Pause Instant Ink link
Need method to click pause plan button
Need method to click pause instant ink link
Need to verify that plan was successfully paused after clicking
May need to wait for confirmation message or status update

This requires either clicking a navigation link/menu item or directly entering a URL. The page should load completely showing the billing cycle period card and related information.

test_steps.raw_action: Check the Billing Cycle Period card.
test_steps.raw_expected_result: Verify the plan pause information is not displayed.
test_steps.interpreted_action: Locate and examine the Billing Cycle Period card component on the Print and Payment History page. At this point in the test flow (before the 32-day shift), the card should NOT display any plan pause information. This is verifying the initial state before the billing cycle advances.

sub_actions.action: Verify plan pause information is not displayed

test_steps.raw_action: Even shift 32 days and trigger billing charge.
test_steps.interpreted_action: Advance the system date/time by 32 days to simulate the passage of time and trigger the next billing cycle charge. This is likely done through a backend time manipulation utility, API call, or database update rather than actual UI interaction. The purpose is to move the subscription into the next billing period where the pause plan information should become visible.

test_data_needed.constraints: Must be 32 days to trigger next billing cycle
test_data_needed.used_for: Time shift for billing cycle simulation

assertions.assertion_description: Verify that time shift operation completed successfully
Verify that billing charge was triggered successfully

wait_conditions.wait_description: Wait for billing charge processing to complete after time shift

error_scenarios.error_indicator: API returns error status
Billing status unchanged

dependencies.dependency_detail: Must verify initial state before time shift

At this point in the test (after pause plan is active but before any printing), the value should show '0 of 10(Pause Plan) used', indicating that 0 out of 10 complimentary pages have been used during the pause plan period.

test_steps.raw_action: Print some pages(less than the plan, such as 6 pages).
test_steps.interpreted_action: Trigger a print job that prints a number of pages less than the complimentary pages limit (10 pages in pause plan). The example given is 6 pages. This simulates actual printer usage and should update the page count and progress bar. This may be done through actual printer simulation, API call to register printed pages, or backend data manipulation.

test_data_needed.constraints: Must be less than complimentary pages limit (10)

test_steps.interpreted_action: Refresh the Print and Payment History page (or dashboard page) to see the updated page usage after printing 6 pages. The Complimentary pages progress bar should now show visual progress (filled with black color) and the value should update to '6 of 10 used'.

test_steps.raw_action: Continue to print some pages(more than the plan, such as 15 pages).
test_steps.interpreted_action: Trigger another print job that brings the total printed pages to more than the complimentary pages limit. The example is 15 pages total (meaning 9 more pages after the initial 6). This should trigger the 'Additional pages' tracking since usage exceeds the 10-page complimentary limit.

sub_actions.action: Trigger print job for additional pages

test_data_needed.constraints: Total pages (6 + 9 = 15) must exceed complimentary limit (10)

Now both the 'Complimentary pages' progress bar (showing full usage of 10 pages) and a new 'Additional pages' progress bar (showing the 5 pages over the limit) should be displayed.

flow_sequence.action_summary: Navigate to Print and Payment History page
Verify initial state - plan pause info not displayed
Shift time by 32 days and trigger billing
Navigate back and verify plan pause info now displayed
Verify complimentary pages progress bar and initial state (0 of 10)
Refresh and verify updated complimentary pages (6 of 10)
Refresh and verify both progress bars displayed
Verify additional pages progress bar details (yellow, 5 of 10)
Verify complimentary pages full (10 of 10) and total pages (15)

flow_analysis.critical_path: Navigate to page → Verify initial state → Time shift → Verify pause info displayed → Verify complimentary pages initial → Print pages under limit → Verify update → Print pages over limit → Verify both progress bars → Verify all details → Verify totals

error_paths.trigger: Print and Payment History page fails to load

error_paths.expected_behavior: Page counts not updated, test fails

Print simulation API or utility available

automation_notes.complexity_justification: This test case is extremely complex due to multiple factors: (1) Requires backend time manipulation (32-day shift) which is not standard UI automation, (2) Requires print job simulation via API or backend, (3) Involves multiple state changes and data dependencies across steps, (4) Requires visual verification of progress bars with specific colors (black vs yellow), (5) Requires responsive testing across desktop/mobile/tablet with different interaction patterns (hover vs click), (6) Involves tooltip interactions which can be timing-sensitive, (7) Requires CSS property verification for colors, (8) Includes visual regression testing with screenshot comparison, (9) Has complex preconditions requiring specific subscription state setup, (10) Involves multiple page refreshes and state synchronization. This test case combines functional, visual, and responsive testing with backend data manipulation.

risks.risk: Backend time manipulation may not be available in all test environments
risks.mitigation: Verify time shift API availability in environment setup. Consider alternative approaches like mocking system time or using test-specific time endpoints.

risks.risk: Print job simulation requires special backend API or test utilities
risks.mitigation: Ensure print simulation API is available and documented. May need coordination with backend team to expose test-only endpoints.

risks.risk: Progress bar color verification may be fragile due to CSS variations
risks.mitigation: Use robust color comparison that handles RGB/hex/color name variations. Consider using color ranges rather than exact matches.

risks.risk: Tooltip interactions are timing-sensitive and may be flaky
risks.mitigation: Implement robust waits for tooltip appearance. Add retry logic for tooltip interactions. Consider increasing timeouts for tooltip-related steps.

risks.risk: Responsive testing requires multiple browser viewport changes which can be slow
risks.mitigation: Consider running responsive tests in parallel or as separate test runs. Cache viewport configurations.

risks.risk: Visual regression testing requires baseline image management
risks.mitigation: Implement proper baseline image versioning and update process. Use visual testing tools with built-in baseline management (Applitools, Percy).

risks.risk: Test has many dependencies on previous steps - failure in early step blocks entire test
risks.mitigation: Consider breaking into smaller independent tests where possible. Implement proper error handling and cleanup to prevent cascading failures.

risks.risk: Page refresh timing may vary causing data sync issues
risks.mitigation: Implement robust waits after page refresh. Verify data is updated before proceeding with assertions. Consider polling for expected state.

risks.risk: Device-specific interaction logic (hover vs click) adds complexity
risks.mitigation: Abstract device-specific interactions into utility functions. Use configuration to determine device type and interaction method.

risks.risk: Test requires specific subscription state that may be hard to set up
risks.mitigation: Use API-based setup to create required subscription state. Implement setup verification before test execution. Consider using dedicated test accounts.

recommendations: Implement comprehensive API helper layer for all backend operations (time shift, print simulation, subscription management)
Use Page Object Model with separate page classes for PrintAndPaymentHistoryPage and InstantInkDashboardPage
Create dedicated utility functions for color verification, tooltip interactions, and responsive testing
Implement robust wait strategies with explicit waits for all dynamic elements (progress bars, tooltips, updated values)
Use visual regression testing tool (Applitools, Percy, or similar) for screenshot comparison in step 20
Abstract device-specific interactions (hover vs click) into utility functions that check device type
Implement proper test data setup and teardown using API calls to ensure clean state
Use configuration files to manage environment-specific settings (URLs, timeouts, device types)
Consider breaking this test into smaller focused tests: (1) Pause plan display test, (2) Complimentary pages tracking test, (3) Additional pages tracking test, (4) Visual/responsive test
Implement retry logic for flaky interactions like tooltips and page refreshes
Use data-driven approach for responsive testing with parameterized viewport sizes
Implement proper error handling and cleanup to prevent test environment pollution
Add detailed logging at each step to aid debugging of complex multi-step flow
Consider using test fixtures or hooks for complex precondition setup
Implement screenshot capture on all assertion failures for debugging
special_handling_notes: This test requires backend time manipulation capability - verify this is available in test environment before execution
Print job simulation must be done via API or backend utility - cannot be done through standard UI automation
Tooltip interactions require device-specific handling - desktop uses hover, mobile/tablet uses click
Progress bar color verification requires CSS property checking - may need custom assertion methods
Visual regression testing (step 20) requires visual testing tool integration - not standard Selenium capability
Responsive testing requires viewport resizing and re-verification of elements at each size
Test has complex state dependencies - each step depends on previous steps completing successfully
Page refresh steps require careful timing to ensure backend data is synchronized before verification
Test involves both UI verification and backend API calls - requires hybrid automation approach
Preconditions require specific subscription state setup - may need dedicated test data or API setup
Test case includes screenshot reference (index.php?/attachments/get/35447537) - this should be used as visual baseline
Test is categorized as 'Visual (Ux) + functional' - requires both functional and visual validation
Analysis status mentions 'To be discussed' items - may need clarification on specific printer requirements and manual execution steps
automation_notes.framework_compatibility_notes: This test case analysis is structured for the Aura framework. All component names follow framework conventions (snake_case for elements/methods, PascalCase for page objects). The test requires significant backend integration capabilities including time manipulation and print simulation APIs. Visual testing will require integration with visual regression testing tools. Responsive testing will require viewport management utilities. The complexity level suggests this test should be implemented by senior automation engineers with experience in hybrid UI/API testing.

notes: This is a comprehensive analysis of test case C44873414 for HP Instant Ink Billing Cycle Period card functionality. The test case is highly complex, involving time manipulation, print simulation, visual verification, and responsive testing. The analysis has identified 21 distinct test steps (including 2 inferred steps for visual and responsive verification), 47 unique UI elements, 35 action methods, 21 assertions, 13 utility functions, and 10 API helpers needed for complete automation. The test requires both UI automation and backend API integration. Special attention is needed for device-specific interactions, color verification, tooltip handling, and visual regression testing. The preconditions are complex and require specific subscription state setup. This test case should be considered a high-priority, high-complexity automation candidate requiring experienced automation engineers and robust test infrastructure.
